perturbation.original_text: 10.2 DEVELOPER Developer shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless Client, its members owners, officers, employees, independent contractors and agents, from and against all losses, claims, liabilities or damages and any related costs and expenses, including attorneys' fees and costs arising out of, or in any way related to any claim or action against Client arising out of or in any way related to: (a) Developer's material breach of his Agreement; (b) Developer's gross negligence or willful misconduct; (c) De eloper's act or omission constituting a violation of applicable federal, state, local law or regulation; or (d) any claim made against Client asserting a violation of any third party intellectual property right pertaining to the Subject Program.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2 DEVELOPER Developer shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless Client, its members owners, officers, employees, independent contractors and agents, from and against all losses, claims, liabilities or damages and any related costs and expenses, excluding attorneys' fees and costs, arising out of or in any way related to any claim or action against Client arising out of or in any way related to: (a) Developer's material breach of his Agreement; (b) Developer's gross negligence or willful misconduct; (c) De eloper's act or omission constituting a violation of applicable federal, state, local law or regulation; or (d) any claim made against Client asserting a violation of any third party intellectual property right pertaining to the Subject Program.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'including attorneys' fees and costs' from the indemnification clause, the contract contradicts the general principle that prevailing parties are entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees and costs in a breach of contract action. This weakens the client's legal recourse and increases their financial risk in case of a dispute in Washington state.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington's principle on awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party in a contract dispute where the contract provides for it, or where statutory or common law dictates.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 4.84.330 states that in any action on a contract or lease, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or lease or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees and costs. By excluding attorneys' fees and costs from the developer's indemnification obligations, the modified contract contradicts this statute, potentially forcing the client to bear their own legal expenses even if they prevail in a dispute caused by the developer's actions, thus failing to provide the legal protection expected under Washington law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 4.84.330: Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties—Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees—Waiver prohibited. Print RCWs > Title 4 > Chapter 4.84 > Section 4.84.330 4.84.320 << 4.84.330 >> 4.84.340 PDF RCW 4.84.330 Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties — Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees — Waiver prohibited. In any action on a contract or lease entered into after September 21, 1977, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or lease or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees in addition to costs and necessary disbursements. Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease which is entered into after September 21, 1977. Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void. As used in this section "prevailing party" means the party in whose favor final judgment is rendered. [ 2011 c 336 s 131 ; 1977 ex.s. c 203 s 1 .]

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts RCW 4.84.330. By removing 'including attorneys' fees and costs' from the indemnification clause, the contract now excludes the developer's obligation to cover these fees, even if the client prevails in a dispute arising from the developer's actions. RCW 4.84.330 states that 'the prevailing party...shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees in addition to costs and necessary disbursements'. The changed text directly undermines this entitlement, forcing the client to bear legal expenses, and contradicting the law's intent.

perturbation.original_text: 12.1 CHOICE OF LAW AND VENUE This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Washington without regards for its conflict of laws principle. The Parties will conduct friendly negotiations to resolve any dispute arising from this Agreement, including mediation if requested by either Party. Should mediation fail, each party consents to the personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in King County, Washington. If there is a dispute between the Parties relating to this Agreement, the Party substantially prevailing will be entitled to recover all costs and expenses of any subsequent proceeding (including trial, appellate, and arbitration proceedings), including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs incurred therein.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.1 CHOICE OF LAW AND VENUE This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Washington without regards for its conflict of laws principle. The Parties will conduct friendly negotiations to resolve any dispute arising from this Agreement. Should friendly negotiations fail, each party consents to the personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in King County, Washington. If there is a dispute between the Parties relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear their own legal expenses.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the prevailing party's entitlement to recover costs, expenses, and attorneys' fees, this clause contradicts the general principle allowing for recovery of such costs in contract disputes, especially in Washington State where contractual fee-shifting provisions are generally enforced. This increases the financial risk for both parties in any legal dispute arising from the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington's principle on awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party in a contract dispute where the contract provides for it, or where statutory or common law dictates.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 4.84.330 ensures that where a contract provides for attorney's fees to one party, the prevailing party is entitled to reasonable fees and costs. The modification removes the clause which allows the prevailing party to be entitled to recover all costs, expenses and attorneys fees. Removing this right undermines the statute's intent by forcing parties to bear their own legal expenses, even when they are successful in enforcing their contractual rights.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts RCW 4.84.330. The original contract allowed the prevailing party to recover costs, expenses, and attorneys' fees. The changed text states that each party shall bear their own legal expenses. This contradicts RCW 4.84.330, which states, "In any action on a contract or lease entered into after September 21, 1977, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party... shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees in addition to costs and necessary disbursements." By removing the provision for the prevailing party to recover fees, the modified contract attempts to circumvent the law's intention, making the contradiction explicit and impactful. The altered clause would probably not hold up in court.
